questions.question: Ultrasound refers to sound with frequencies:
questions.explanation: Ultrasound has frequencies above the upper limit of human hearing ($20\text{ kHz}$).

questions.question: Infrasound refers to sound with frequencies:
questions.explanation: Infrasound has frequencies below the lower limit of human hearing ($20\text{ Hz}$).

questions.question: Ultrasound is used in medicine primarily to:
options: Create images of internal organs and fetuses
questions.explanation: Medical ultrasound sends high-frequency pulses into the body and uses the returning echoes to create real-time images.

questions.question: Which animal uses ultrasound for navigation and hunting?
questions.explanation: Bats emit high-frequency ultrasound pulses and listen for echoes to navigate and locate prey in the dark.

questions.question: Which animal is known to communicate using infrasound over long distances?
questions.explanation: Elephants produce infrasound that can travel several kilometers, allowing them to communicate across vast distances.

questions.question: Which of the following is a natural source of infrasound?
questions.explanation: Volcanic eruptions, earthquakes, and severe storms all generate powerful infrasound waves below $20\text{ Hz}$.

questions.question: Ultrasound is used in industry for cleaning delicate objects because it:
options: Heats the cleaning liquid to a boil to melt stubborn dirt away.
Produces high-frequency vibrations that dislodge stuck particles.
Creates a temporary vacuum layer that strips grease off surfaces.
Changes the chemical composition of dirt to dissolve it completely.
questions.explanation: High-frequency ultrasound waves traveling through a liquid create rapid vibrations and microscopic bubbles. When these bubbles collapse, they release tiny bursts of mechanical energy that dislodge dirt from delicate or hard-to-reach surfaces without scratching them.

questions.question: Humans cannot hear ultrasound waves primarily because:
options: The physical speed of the wave is too fast for the ear to capture.
The frequency of the wave is too high for the ear structures to detect.
The amplitude of the wave is too low to deliver enough sound energy.
The wavelength of the wave is too long to fit into the auditory canal.
questions.explanation: The human range of hearing is limited to frequencies between $20\text{ Hz}$ and $20,000\text{ Hz}$ ($20\text{ kHz}$). Because ultrasound consists of sound waves with frequencies higher than $20,000\text{ Hz}$, our ear structures cannot vibrate fast enough to detect them, regardless of how loud or fast the wave travels.

questions.question: Infrasound waves can be successfully detected by using:
options: Standard human ears operating under quiet room conditions.
Specialized microphones designed to record low pressure shifts.
Ordinary household microphones built for typical voice recording.
Standard medical stethoscope tools placed directly against a surface.
questions.explanation: Infrasound consists of sound waves with frequencies below $20\text{ Hz}$, which is below the lower limit of human hearing. Because these waves have long wavelengths and represent slow pressure variations, they require specialized microbarometers or infrasound microphones that can track very low-frequency pressure changes.

questions.question: A medical ultrasound transducer emits waves with a wavelength of $0.77\text{ mm}$ in tissue. If the speed of sound in tissue is $1,540\text{ m/s}$, what is the frequency?
Dolphins use echolocation clicks with frequencies up to $150\text{ kHz}$. What is the wavelength of a $150\text{ kHz}$ click in water? ($v = 1,500\text{ m/s}$)
An earthquake produces infrasound at $10\text{ Hz}$. If the speed of sound in air is $340\text{ m/s}$, what is the wavelength?
questions.explanation: $\lambda = v/f = 340/10 = 34\text{ m}$. Infrasound has very long wavelengths.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered ultrasound, infrasound, and their applications!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the frequency ranges and which animals use ultrasound vs. infrasound.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the applications of ultrasound and the sources of infrasound.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: ultrasound > $20\text{ kHz}$, infrasound < $20\text{ Hz}$, and their real-world uses.
